sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Peripheral IV cannulation provides access for fluids, medications, and blood sampling when clinically indicated. In prehospital care it should support the case, not prolong scene time or distract from more urgent priorities such as transport, airway, or haemorrhage control.

sections.title: Site Selection
sections.content:
- Choose a site that balances urgency, likelihood of success, required flow, patient comfort, and securement
- Larger and more proximal veins may be preferable for resuscitation, but not if repeated failed attempts will delay care
- Avoid compromised limbs or sites where the line is unlikely to remain functional

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Prepare equipment, choose the target vein, and optimise the site with positioning, warmth, or tourniquet as appropriate

2. Clean the site and cannulate using standard aseptic technique

3. Once flashback is obtained, advance and thread the cannula carefully to secure intravascular placement

4. Release the tourniquet, connect extension tubing or a cap, and flush to confirm patency

5. Secure the line well and label it according to local practice

6. Stop or change strategy early if repeated attempts are failing or are delaying more important care

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- The best cannula is the one that works for the actual clinical need; bigger is not always better if it lowers first-pass success
- Confirm patency before giving medications or starting a fluid bolus
- Escalate early to IO or another strategy in a crashing patient rather than persisting with multiple failed IV attempts
- Document the site, gauge, number of attempts, and whether the line functioned reliably
